channel.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India
channel.description: World News Today: Read the latest world news headlines, Current International breaking news world wide. In depth analysis and top news headlines world wide.

image.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India

item.title: Quote of the day by Confucius: 'If I am walking with two other men, each of them will serve...'
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Larry Ellison once struggled to pay his electricity bills. Now, he has written a $300 million cheque to...
item.description: Once grappling with utility bills for his workshop, tech magnate Larry Ellison transformed his fortunes by acquiring 98 percent of a Hawaiian island for a staggering $300 million. Now residing full-time on Lanai, he envisions a luxury destination coupled with a sustainable community. Despite fluctuations in his wealth driven by market trends, Oracle's cloud operations showcase remarkable strength amid ongoing challenges.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: IShowSpeed performs 'Champions' at FIFA World Cup 2026 final closing ceremony
item.description: IShowSpeed performed his song "Champions" at the FIFA World Cup 2026 final closing ceremony. He became the first content creator to perform live at this major global sporting event. His viral World Cup anthem gained significant popularity among football fans worldwide. FIFA secured rights to his song, which became a recognized tournament track. This performance highlighted the growing influence of online creators on international stages.
item.dc:creator: Sumit Roy

item.title: MacKenzie Scott once fixed a broken tooth with glue. Now she's giving $20 million to struggling teenagers
item.description: Philanthropist MacKenzie Scott gave a record $20 million to Active Minds. This donation addresses the critical youth mental health crisis in America. Scott's past financial struggles influenced her generous giving approach. The funding will expand mental health programs across schools and universities. Active Minds aims to reduce stigma and improve support accessibility for young people.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Quote of the day by Aristotle: "The ideal man bears the accidents of life with dignity and…" - a powerful lesson on resilience, character and how people respond when life does not go according to plan
item.description: Life's unpredictable events require a dignified and graceful response from individuals. Aristotle's philosophy emphasizes maintaining composure during both good and bad fortune. Dignity involves retaining self-worth while experiencing emotions like grief or fear. Grace means responding firmly to injustice without succumbing to anger. This approach focuses on how one handles setbacks, not on avoiding them.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Thrown into battle in two weeks': Russia plans 500,000-strong mobilisation, Ukraine claims
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Iran's unseen Supreme Leader may turn to Putin as first foreign contact amid war
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Pay quarterly, not yearly: Saudi Arabia introduces 3-month residency permit renewal option for domestic workers
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Scientists studied found flies rebuild their biological clock into a separate seasonal mode
item.description: In a fascinating discovery, scientists have found that fruit flies adapt their internal biological clocks to survive winter by employing a genetic mechanism known as the winter lock. This adaptation allows them to remain inactive until warmer weather arrives, showcasing a seasonal shift in their molecular structure. These insights not only open avenues for innovative pest control strategies but also enhance our understanding of seasonal variations in human health issues.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Mediterranean seagrass survives in dim seabed light by expanding its photosynthetic antenna
item.description: Scientists have discovered how the Mediterranean seagrass Posidonia oceanicahas adapted its photosynthetic system to survive in the dim, blue-green light of the seabed. The plant captures more light while moving energy more efficiently by altering its photosynthetic machinery. Researchers say the findings could help develop crop plants that make better use of limited sunlight under dense canopies.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Residents kept reporting Dolphins. Underwater microphones revealed their visits
item.description: Scientists from Kyoto University used underwater microphones to monitor dolphins in two bays along Japan's Sea of Japan coast. They found dolphin sounds only about once every 10 days, suggesting the animals visit these waters occasionally. The study also shows that long-term underwater listening can help scientists better understand and conserve marine life.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Florida is planting 15,000 milkweeds along highways to help monarch butterflies
item.description: Scientists and transport officials in Florida have planted 15,000 milkweed plants along roadsides to help monarch butterflies survive and reproduce. Milkweed is the only plant on which monarchs lay their eggs, making it essential for their life cycle. The project also supports bees and other pollinators while testing whether roadside habitats can become long-term wildlife corridors across the state.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Elisha Cuthbert has sold her longtime LA home for $1.93 million after cutting the asking price
item.description: Hollywood actress Elisha Cuthbert has sold her Los Angeles home for $1.93 million after several unsuccessful attempts to find a buyer. The property was sold shortly after a final price cut. The sale comes as Cuthbert returns to acting following a four-year break, during which she focused on raising her two children with husband Dion Phaneuf.
item.dc:creator: TOI Real Estate Desk

item.title: Scientists discover a ‘third cell’ that helps regrow hair
item.description: In a groundbreaking development, Japanese researchers have unveiled a pivotal third cell integral to the regeneration of hair follicles. This finding holds the potential to reverse conditions like baldness and thinning hair by collaborating with two established cell types. Successful experiments conducted on mice demonstrated the complete cycle of hair growth, paving the way for pioneering regenerative treatments aimed at combating hair loss.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: The real-life Gilmore Girls house from the pilot episode is for sale for $2.7 million in Ontario, where Unionville first stood in for Stars Hollow
item.description: A historic Unionville home, which served as Lorelai and Rory Gilmore’s house in the pilot episode, is now for sale. This property, listed at $3.88 million CAD, offers a tangible connection to the beloved show. The surrounding neighborhood also featured as key Stars Hollow locations, enhancing its appeal. The house, built around 1875, blends Victorian charm with modern amenities for comfortable living.
item.dc:creator: TOI Lifestyle Desk

item.title: Scientists identify rare meteorite behind dinosaur extinction
item.description: Scientists have identified a rare CO chondrite meteorite as the impactor that caused dinosaur extinction. This space rock, which struck Earth 66 million years ago, wiped out most life. Advanced nickel isotope analysis traced the meteorite's chemical signature to the deadly impact event. The rare nature of this projectile highlights the dinosaurs' extreme misfortune. This impact created the Chicxulub crater and led to a mass extinction.
item.dc:creator: TOI Lifestyle Desk

item.title: Tiny plastics in drinking water may help harmful bacteria resist disinfectants
item.description: New research reveals nanoplastics can strengthen harmful bacterial biofilms in water systems. These tiny plastic particles help microbes survive disinfection treatments more effectively. This makes maintaining clean drinking water infrastructure significantly more challenging. The presence of nanoplastics may contribute to the rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. Understanding these interactions is crucial for public health and water management efforts.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Astronomers just spotted erythrulose, the tastiest sugar found in some berries here on Earth, and why this discovery could change how we search for life in space
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Earth has regulated its climate for 60 million years through a hidden ocean feedback
item.description: A new study has identified a natural process that may have helped keep Earth's climate stable for the past 60 million years. Researchers found that changes in sea level controlled the amount of phosphate reaching the oceans, affecting marine life, carbon burial beneath the seafloor and the level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere. The findings offer a new explanation for how Earth naturally regulated long-term warming and cooling.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Scientists use teeth to trace childhood journeys of Africans freed from slave ships
item.description: A new study has uncovered the childhood origins of 152 Africans rescued from illegal slave ships and taken to Saint Helena nearly 200 years ago. Using tooth chemistry, ancient DNA and historical records, researchers found they came from different parts of Central Africa, with many forced to travel long distances before reaching the coast. The findings help restore the identities of people whose lives were erased by the transatlantic slave trade.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk
